title: ICS-200 For Initial Incident Command
stem: A regional emergency medical services (EMS) strike-team leader has completed introductory Incident Command System (ICS) training but will now supervise initial response resources at small multiagency incidents. Which additional training target best matches that role?

options.A: Basic Incident Command System for Initial Response (ICS-200), because it applies command concepts to initial response resources.
options.B: Emergency Support Function #8, because it organizes federal public health and medical support after a declared disaster.
options.C: Hospital evacuation operations training, because it focuses on moving patients and maintaining facility continuity during evacuation.
options.D: Public information officer training, because it prepares leaders to coordinate incident messaging and rumor control.

rationale: Basic Incident Command System for Initial Response (ICS-200) is the best match for a supervisor managing initial response resources. B is a federal public-health support function, not initial incident command training. C addresses facility evacuation rather than field command. D is useful for messaging roles but too narrow for supervising response resources.
citation: Catlett CL, Jenkins JL, Millin MG. Disaster preparedness and management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:286.

title: Bariatric Airway Reserve
stem: A bariatric patient with pneumonia needs assisted ventilation during a long stair carry. Despite preoxygenation, oxygen saturation falls rapidly during brief pauses in ventilation, and mask seal is difficult. Which explanation best fits this airway risk?

options.A: Obesity mainly increases lifting risk; desaturation during apnea is usually unrelated to body habitus.
options.B: Redundant upper-airway tissue and reduced respiratory reserve increase difficult mask ventilation and rapid desaturation risk.
options.C: Bariatric patients usually tolerate apnea better because larger lung volumes create a larger oxygen reservoir.
options.D: Rapid desaturation suggests occult pneumothorax until proven otherwise, even when mask ventilation is difficult.

rationale: Bariatric patients may have reduced respiratory reserve and difficult mask ventilation, so airway planning should anticipate rapid desaturation. A underestimates the airway problem. C reverses the physiology. D overanchors on pneumothorax despite the immediate mask-ventilation problem.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:480-481.

title: System 1 Reasoning Trap
stem: A senior paramedic sees a diaphoretic patient breathing rapidly after an argument and immediately says, "panic attack." The monitor then shows hypotension and a wide-complex tachycardia. Which teaching point best addresses the decision error?

options.A: Experienced clinicians should avoid intuitive impressions because System 1 reasoning is unsafe in EMS.
options.B: Pattern recognition should be trusted when the first impression is made by the most experienced clinician on scene.
options.C: Fast intuitive reasoning is useful, but clinicians must slow down when discordant facts threaten the initial pattern.
options.D: Formal algorithms eliminate the need to recognize when an initial intuitive diagnosis may be wrong.

rationale: System 1 reasoning is useful but can mislead when new facts conflict with the first impression. A rejects useful clinical pattern recognition. B overtrusts the initial impression. D overstates algorithms and ignores bedside reassessment.
citation: Bigham BL, Nolan B, Patterson PD. Patient safety culture. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:417.

topic: Prehospital stroke identification and severity scales
title: Stroke Mimic Glucose Check
stem: A 68-year-old with diabetes has sudden aphasia and right arm weakness. The crew obtains a positive stroke screen while preparing for transport. Which additional step should be built into the protocol without delaying definitive stroke care?

options.A: Obtain two points of intravenous access before departure so thrombolytic therapy can begin immediately on arrival.
options.B: Complete a full medication reconciliation on scene to determine whether antiplatelet therapy is already prescribed.
options.C: Repeat the stroke screen after packaging and again after loading before notifying the receiving stroke center.
options.D: Check glucose promptly because hypoglycemia can mimic focal stroke and changes immediate treatment.

rationale: Glucose should be checked promptly because hypoglycemia can mimic focal neurologic deficits and is immediately treatable without delaying stroke care. A and B may be useful later but are not the time-sensitive field step. C can support reassessment but should not delay stroke-center notification or glucose testing.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:172-173.

topic: Goal of minimizing scene time for severe trauma
title: Penetrating Trauma Fluid Timing
stem: A patient with a stab wound to the upper abdomen is pale, confused, and has a weak radial pulse. The ambulance is ready and the trauma center is 11 minutes away. What should the crew prioritize?

options.A: Move toward transport while controlling external bleeding and give limited resuscitation en route if needed.
options.B: Place two large-bore intravenous lines during packaging, but do not leave scene until a liter of crystalloid has infused.
options.C: Complete a head-to-toe secondary survey and detailed abdominal exam before departure to better localize hemorrhage.
options.D: Defer vascular access entirely during transport because any preoperative fluid worsens outcome in penetrating trauma.

rationale: Unstable penetrating trauma should move toward definitive hemorrhage control; access and limited resuscitation should not delay transport. B delays transport for crystalloid. C postpones departure for a low-yield survey. D is too absolute because vascular access and limited resuscitation can still be appropriate.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:259.

title: Public Access Defibrillation Law Variation
stem: A medical director is helping a regional airport expand public access defibrillation. The airport asks whether state law requires specific maintenance logs and what liability protection applies to lay rescuers. Which response is most defensible?

options.A: Use the national automated external defibrillator (AED) template because public access defibrillation law is uniform across states.
options.B: Review the applicable state public access defibrillation statute and build the program around its training, maintenance, and liability provisions.
options.C: Let each business choose its own approach because AED programs are outside EMS medical oversight once devices are public.
options.D: Avoid lay-rescuer AED placement unless an EMS clinician can be physically present at every device location.

rationale: Public access defibrillation programs must account for state-specific legal requirements. A assumes laws are uniform nationally. C treats a clinical program as purely educational and ignores legal structure. D would make implementation impractical without adding the needed legal analysis.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:250.

topic: Integration of EMS, community public resources, mental health resources, and social services
title: Coalition Coordination During Surge
stem: During a regional heat emergency, hospitals need real-time bed status, public health needs cooling-center information, and EMS needs guidance on patient distribution. Which structure best supports this cross-sector coordination?

options.A: Each ambulance crew should call its preferred hospital, allowing patient distribution to emerge from routine radio traffic.
options.B: The largest hospital should control all EMS destinations because it has the most clinicians available.
options.C: Use a health care coalition or medical coordination center model linking hospitals, public safety, public health, government, and relief partners.
options.D: The EMS agency should coordinate internally and notify partners after transports are complete.

rationale: Coalition and medical coordination structures help agencies share information and coordinate scarce resources during surge. A is too isolated. B makes transport agencies passive rather than integrated partners. D is hospital-centric and misses cross-agency coordination.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:314.

title: PDSA Small Test Of Change
stem: A service wants to improve epinephrine timing in cardiac arrest. One station will trial a medication-layout checklist for two weeks, measure timing, revise the checklist, and then decide whether to spread it. What method does this best represent?

options.A: A root-cause analysis, because any medication delay should be investigated only after a sentinel event.
options.B: A final policy implementation, because one positive station trial proves system-wide effectiveness.
options.C: A disciplinary audit, because medication timing is primarily an individual compliance issue.
options.D: A Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle using a small test of change before broader adoption.

rationale: Plan-Do-Study-Act cycles begin with small tests, measurement, learning, and a decision to adapt, adopt, or abandon. A skips measurement. B turns the method into a final proof trial. C treats a small change cycle as permanent implementation before learning.
citation: Bourn SS, Mackey KE, Redlener M. Defining, measuring, and improving quality. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:437.

title: Tactical Field Care Tradeoff
stem: A tactical team reaches a wounded officer behind cover. The threat is reduced but not gone, extraction is possible in 2 minutes, and the casualty has a compressible thigh hemorrhage. Which action best fits tactical field care?

options.A: Control the life-threatening extremity bleeding quickly while balancing treatment time against extraction risk.
options.B: Begin full routine trauma assessment because once behind cover the sequence should match ordinary EMS care.
options.C: Withhold all care until the scene is completely secure, regardless of hemorrhage severity.
options.D: Start intravenous access and spinal motion restriction before hemorrhage control because those steps are routine trauma priorities.

rationale: Tactical field care requires balancing treatable life threats against the threat and extraction environment. B delays hemorrhage control despite a rapidly correctable life threat. C imposes a routine trauma sequence that may be unsafe tactically. D overprioritizes an advanced procedure before evacuation.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:378.

title: Viral Hemorrhagic Fever Precautions
stem: EMS is called for a traveler recently returned from a viral hemorrhagic fever outbreak region. The patient has fever, severe fatigue, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and now mucosal bleeding on day 5 of illness. Which EMS action is most appropriate before and during transport?

options.A: Use routine gloves and a surgical mask, because bleeding is already present and additional precautions will not change responder risk.
options.B: Use special-pathogen precautions, isolate the patient, limit personnel, and notify the receiving facility and public health authorities.
options.C: Use droplet precautions only, because viral hemorrhagic fevers are managed like uncomplicated influenza during EMS transport.
options.D: Transport immediately without advance notification, because destination planning should wait until laboratory confirmation is available.

rationale: The exposure history plus compatible evolving illness should trigger viral hemorrhagic fever special-pathogen precautions, patient isolation, personnel limitation, and early notification. A underprotects responders. C treats the illness like routine respiratory infection. D delays system notification until after a preventable exposure risk has already occurred.
citation: MacDonald RD. Infectious and communicable diseases. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:217.

title: Physician Field Procedure Governance
stem: A medical director wants an EMS physician response option for rare field procedures such as perimortem cesarean delivery, escharotomy, or field amputation. What should precede implementation?

options.A: Credential any physician already privileged in an emergency department to perform field procedures when they arrive on scene.
options.B: Exclude rare physician procedures from the response model because they are outside ordinary paramedic scope.
options.C: Written guidelines defining indications, credentialing, equipment, legal authority, operational integration, and review for rare physician field interventions.
options.D: Rely on online approval for the first case, then decide equipment, training, and review requirements afterward.

rationale: Rare physician field procedures require written governance for indications, credentialing, equipment, legal authority, operations, and review before launch. A grants overly broad authority to any physician on scene. B forbids procedures that may be appropriate in a defined physician-response model. D defers training and equipment decisions until the first case, which is unsafe.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:13.

title: Severe TBI Airway Quality Dependency
stem: A severe traumatic brain injury patient has a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 6 but is oxygenating with two-person bag-valve-mask ventilation and capnography. A protocol committee is debating automatic rapid sequence intubation for all such patients. Which concern is most important?

options.A: Use endotracheal intubation only when bag-valve-mask ventilation fails, because intubation is otherwise harmful in traumatic brain injury.
options.B: Continue bag-valve-mask ventilation for every transport, because it avoids all ventilation complications from intubation.
options.C: Automatic intubation of trauma patients with Glasgow Coma Scale scores less than 8 is compliant with Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) guidance, so post-intubation ventilation quality is not a protocol-design concern.
options.D: Automatic intubation based only on Glasgow Coma Scale may harm patients if post-intubation ventilation and end-tidal carbon dioxide control are poor.

rationale: Automatic intubation based only on Glasgow Coma Scale can harm patients if oxygenation, ventilation, and end-tidal carbon dioxide control are poor after the tube is placed. A is too categorical because intubation may be appropriate. B is also too categorical because bag-valve-mask ventilation is not always superior. C mistakes a general airway indication for proof that post-intubation ventilation quality no longer matters.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:239.

topic: Management and resuscitation of multi-trauma patient
title: Permissive Hypotension Boundary
stem: A young adult has a penetrating abdominal wound, no head injury signs, a palpable radial pulse, and a 15-minute transport to surgery. Which resuscitation target is most appropriate before hemorrhage control?

options.A: Use limited resuscitation aimed at maintaining perfusion rather than normalizing blood pressure before surgical control.
options.B: Give repeated crystalloid boluses until blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg before surgical hemorrhage control.
options.C: Withhold all resuscitation even if the radial pulse disappears and mental status worsens.
options.D: Delay departure until blood pressure has normalized because transport hypotension is the dominant preventable risk.

rationale: Limited resuscitation aimed at maintaining perfusion is appropriate before hemorrhage control in selected penetrating trauma without severe head injury. B overnormalizes blood pressure before definitive control. C withholds resuscitation despite worsening perfusion. D delays needed transport for a nondefinitive endpoint.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:259.

title: Abandonment And Inadequate Assessment
stem: A crew finds a stuporous person outside a bar. Police say he is "a regular drunk." There is no obvious trauma, but he cannot answer questions or walk. What medical director teaching point best fits the risk?

options.A: Police familiarity can support nontransport when no trauma is visible.
options.B: Leaving after an inadequate assessment of altered mental status can constitute grossly negligent abandonment.
options.C: A patient presumed intoxicated can be left if no bystander requests transport.
options.D: A refusal form is enough if the crew documents that the patient was uncooperative.

rationale: Altered mental status requires assessment and appropriate care; presumed intoxication does not excuse abandonment. A substitutes police custody for medical assessment. C relies on a signature when capacity is impaired. D treats a common diagnostic label as permission to withhold care.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:153.

topic: Onsite public safety answering point and dispatch
title: Event Dispatch Responsibilities
stem: At a large race, a spectator collapses in a remote section. The caller needs cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) instructions, event medical units need dispatch, and response times must be captured. Which dispatch function is most complete?

options.A: Relay the call to the nearest ambulance crew and leave response-time tracking to the event organizer.
options.B: Send law enforcement first because medical instructions should wait until trained responders arrive.
options.C: Provide prearrival instructions, dispatch appropriate event resources, and record relevant response times.
options.D: Avoid prearrival instructions because event volunteers may misunderstand CPR directions.

rationale: Dispatch responsibilities include prearrival instructions, resource dispatch, and time capture. A omits the medical-guidance role. B delays instructions until responders arrive. D withholds useful instructions because the call may evolve.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:201.

title: Postpartum Hemorrhage Adjuncts
stem: A critical care transport team is moving a patient with postpartum hemorrhage from a rural hospital. Fundal massage has started, large-bore access is in place, and the patient remains hypotensive. Which plan best fits prehospital hemorrhage care when system capability exists?

options.A: Prioritize vasopressors after access is obtained, while arranging uterotonics and blood only if transfer is delayed.
options.B: Continue massage and crystalloid resuscitation, but defer uterotonics, tranexamic acid, and blood products to the receiving team.
options.C: Pause transfer for bedside reassessment and medication sequencing until the bleeding trend is clearly improving.
options.D: Continue uterine measures, hemorrhagic-shock resuscitation, available adjuncts, and transport to definitive obstetric care.

rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage care combines uterine measures, hemorrhagic-shock resuscitation, available adjuncts, and rapid movement toward definitive obstetric care. A misframes hemorrhagic shock as primarily distributive. B abandons useful uterine measures. C creates an unsafe transport delay while waiting for complete stabilization.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:363-364.

title: BRUE Definition Boundary
stem: A 3-month-old had a 20-second episode of pallor, irregular breathing, and limpness that resolved before EMS arrival. The infant now appears well, with no obvious choking or seizure explanation. Which label best fits the event?

options.A: Brief resolved unexplained event (BRUE).
options.B: Febrile seizure.
options.C: Pediatric syncope.
options.D: Apparent life-threatening event (ALTE).

rationale: The event fits brief resolved unexplained event (BRUE): age younger than 1 year, sudden brief resolved episode, and no explanation after initial assessment. B assumes seizure without fever or persistent postictal findings. C applies an adult-style label rather than the infant-specific construct. D uses the obsolete apparent life-threatening event (ALTE) terminology.
citation: Gross T, Donofrio-Odmann JJ. Pediatric medical priorities. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:442.

title: Emergency Risk Communication Statement
stem: After a chemical odor near a school, social media claims children are dying. EMS has transported two stable patients with throat irritation, and hazardous-materials testing is pending. Which public statement is best?

options.A: No deaths are confirmed; responders are evaluating symptoms and testing the scene, and nearby families should follow official shelter or evacuation instructions.
options.B: We have transported two stable patients with throat irritation; testing is underway, and people nearby should follow public-safety instructions while we update confirmed information.
options.C: The symptoms may represent a toxic release; the chemical name will be announced once crews identify it.
options.D: No incident details will be released until the investigation is complete, so residents should wait for a final report.

rationale: The best statement reports confirmed facts, acknowledges uncertainty, gives protective instructions, and promises updates. A falsely reassures. C speculates about an unconfirmed toxic gas. D creates an information vacuum and invites rumor spread.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:253.

topic: Contact (e.g., Norovirus, MRSA, Hep B, C)
title: Bloodborne Exposure Route
stem: After a call involving a patient with hepatitis C, one emergency medical technician (EMT) touched dried blood with intact skin, another had blood splash into an eye, and a third cleaned the stretcher without gloves. Which exposure requires the most immediate bloodborne-pathogen follow-up?

options.A: Touching dried blood with intact skin, because any visible blood contact requires source-patient testing and prophylaxis.
options.B: Standing within 3 feet of the patient during transport, because hepatitis C spreads by droplet exposure.
options.C: Blood splash to the eye, because mucous-membrane exposure to blood is a meaningful bloodborne-pathogen exposure.
options.D: Cleaning the stretcher without gloves, because equipment contact is automatically equivalent to a needlestick.

rationale: A blood splash to the eye is a meaningful mucous-membrane exposure to blood. A overstates risk from intact-skin contact. B incorrectly treats hepatitis C as a droplet infection. D overgeneralizes environmental cleanup into the same risk as a mucous-membrane or percutaneous exposure.
citation: MacDonald RD. Infectious and communicable diseases. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:208.

title: Pediatric Airway Program Metrics
stem: A service permits pediatric endotracheal intubation and supraglottic airway placement. After a hypoxic event, the medical director reviews the quality program. Which metric set is most appropriate?

options.A: Adult first-pass success and annual equipment checks, because pediatric events are too rare for separate review.
options.B: A yearly skills signoff only, because airway competence is primarily a training issue.
options.C: Number of pediatric airways attempted, because procedure volume alone is the best competence measure.
options.D: Pediatric-specific case review tracking selection, attempts, oxygenation, capnography, complications, and outcomes.

rationale: Pediatric airway programs need pediatric-specific safety review, skill selection, and outcome monitoring. A treats adult success metrics as interchangeable. B makes neonatal and pediatric airways an unmonitored exception. C focuses on equipment count without evaluating patient outcomes or skill performance.
citation: Mandt M, Harris M, Lyng J, et al. Quality management of prehospital pediatric respiratory distress and airway programs. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2022;26(sup1):111-117.

title: Hypoglycemia Treat-And-Release Criteria
stem: After two recurrent hypoglycemia cases in patients released after dextrose while taking long-acting sulfonylureas, an EMS medical director reviews wide variation in nontransport decisions. What should the medical director prioritize?

options.A: Written nontransport criteria with quality review that treat sulfonylurea or other high-recurrence medication causes as exclusion triggers unless a specific medical-control pathway supports release.
options.B: Allow release whenever mental status normalizes after glucose, because response to treatment proves the emergency has resolved.
options.C: Require online medical control for every case but avoid written criteria so decisions remain individualized.
options.D: Use transport refusal forms only, because hypoglycemia nontransport is primarily a legal documentation problem.

rationale: Recurrent hypoglycemia risk, especially with sulfonylureas or other long-acting causes, should be addressed in explicit nontransport criteria and quality review. B ignores recurrence risk. C creates case-by-case variability without durable criteria. D reduces a clinical safety issue to paperwork.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:192.

title: Interventional Study Identification
stem: Researchers assign half of EMS stations to a new sepsis-alert script and leave the other half with usual care, then compare time to antibiotics after hospital arrival. What study category best describes this design?

options.A: Case-control study, because patients are compared by hospital antibiotic timing.
options.B: Interventional study, because the investigators impose a change and study its effects.
options.C: Cross-sectional survey, because the outcome is measured at one point in time.
options.D: Ecologic study, because station-level assignment means no intervention occurred.

rationale: An interventional study imposes an assigned change and evaluates outcomes. A starts with outcomes and looks backward. C measures exposure and outcome at one point in time. D misreads cluster assignment as automatically observational.
citation: Lerner EB, Cone DC, Yealy DM. EMS research basics. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:461.

title: QI Infrastructure And Trust
stem: A quality-improvement committee wants crews to report near-misses. Past reports led to punitive emails and no visible system change. Which first step best addresses the quality infrastructure problem?

options.A: Increase reporting requirements and penalties while launching anonymous aggregate feedback after the reporting rate improves.
options.B: Have supervisors review near-misses confidentially first, then invite frontline staff once trust has improved.
options.C: Build a nonpunitive feedback process with frontline engagement, visible follow-through, and clear separation from reckless-behavior discipline.
options.D: Collect only serious adverse events until the committee can prove near-misses are causing measurable harm.

rationale: A nonpunitive feedback process with frontline engagement and visible follow-through is the best first step to rebuild reporting trust. A increases fear. B removes the people closest to the work. D mistakes low reporting for low risk.
citation: Bourn SS, Mackey KE, Redlener M. Defining, measuring, and improving quality. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:435.

title: Field-Relevant POC Testing
stem: A service is considering a new point-of-care (POC) blood test. It performs well in a hospital lab but is temperature-sensitive, takes 25 minutes, and would not change protocol decisions. Which concern is most important?

options.A: The test should be adopted if laboratory validity is high, even when field conditions are unreliable.
options.B: POC testing is inappropriate in EMS because all diagnostic testing should wait for the emergency department.
options.C: A slow test is acceptable if it produces a printed result for the receiving physician.
options.D: Valid and reliable in EMS conditions, and able to change an action within protocol.

rationale: A useful field point-of-care test must be reliable in EMS conditions and change a decision. A ignores field validity and actionability. B focuses on laboratory performance rather than operational use. C treats any new device as unacceptable instead of evaluating whether it changes care.
citation: Carter AJE. Point-of-care testing in EMS. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:559-560.

title: Human Factors Safer Default
stem: A medication box stores look-alike pediatric epinephrine and saline syringes side by side. Two near-misses occur despite reminders. Which redesign best uses human-factors thinking?

options.A: Separate and label the syringes so the intended medication is more visible and the wrong selection is harder to make.
options.B: Send another memo reminding clinicians to read labels more carefully.
options.C: Require crews to sign a form after each shift confirming that they checked the medication box.
options.D: Move responsibility to the individual clinician because medication selection is a cognitive task.

rationale: Human-factors design reduces error by making the safe action easier, more visible, and harder to bypass. B relies mainly on memory. C adds punishment without redesigning the trap. D depends on vigilance instead of improving the system.
citation: Bigham BL, Nolan B, Patterson PD. Patient safety culture. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:416-417.

title: Community Intervention For CARES Outcomes
stem: A jurisdiction participates in the Cardiac Arrest Registry to Enhance Survival (CARES). Its reports show low bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation, low public automated external defibrillator use, and poor survival with good neurologic outcome after witnessed shockable arrests. Which initiative is most likely to improve patient-oriented outcomes?

options.A: Increase epinephrine protocol compliance through medication checklists and post-call feedback for every cardiac arrest.
options.B: Build community cardiopulmonary resuscitation training, dispatcher-assisted cardiopulmonary resuscitation, and public automated external defibrillator access before ambulance arrival.
options.C: Buy mechanical cardiopulmonary resuscitation devices for all ambulances and track device deployment time as the main outcome.
options.D: Prioritize early advanced airway placement in all arrests and report first-pass success as the main system measure.

rationale: For patient-oriented cardiac arrest outcomes, the strongest system lever in this scenario is earlier bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation and defibrillation before EMS arrival. A may improve process reliability but has less direct evidence for the stated gap. C and D may be reasonable operational metrics in some systems but do not address the low bystander intervention and early defibrillation problem shown in CARES data.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:250.

topic: Integration of EMS, community public resources, mental health resources, and social services
title: Public Health Functions In EMS Collaboration
stem: An EMS agency partners with public health to address repeat asthma calls in substandard housing. They map call clusters, support housing-code policy discussions, and verify referral access. Which public-health function sequence is represented?

options.A: Treatment, transport, and billing.
options.B: Dispatch, response, and destination selection.
options.C: Assessment, policy development, and assurance.
options.D: Credentialing, discipline, and remediation.

rationale: The scenario shows assessment through mapping call clusters, policy development through housing-code discussions, and assurance through referral access. A and B describe EMS operations rather than public-health functions. D describes workforce governance.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:246-249.

title: Intention-To-Treat Analysis
stem: In a randomized EMS trial, several crews assigned to the video-feedback arm never view the feedback, while two usual-care crews obtain similar feedback informally. The investigators analyze patients according to original assignment. What analysis is this?

options.A: Per-protocol analysis, because treatment actually received determines the group.
options.B: As-treated analysis, because crossover is reassigned to the intervention actually used.
options.C: Case-control analysis, because outcome status determines which records are included.
options.D: Intention-to-treat analysis, because subjects remain in their assigned groups regardless of actual treatment received.

rationale: Intention-to-treat analyzes participants in their original assigned groups, preserving the benefits of assignment despite crossover or nonadherence. A would remove nonadherent patients and bias results. B analyzes only treatment actually received. C converts the trial into a per-protocol subgroup analysis.
citation: Newgard CD, Lewis RJ. Statistical concepts for research in emergency medical services. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:507.

title: Dyspnea Anchoring Trap
stem: A patient with dyspnea has wheezing, diaphoresis, chest pressure, and a blood glucose of 420 mg/dL with deep respirations. Which diagnostic approach is best?

options.A: Keep a broad differential while treating immediate threats.
options.B: Treat as asthma because wheezing localizes dyspnea to the lower airways.
options.C: Treat as panic because deep respirations with clear speech are rarely metabolic.
options.D: Treat as heart failure because diaphoresis and chest pressure exclude pulmonary disease.

rationale: Dyspnea requires broad assessment while immediate threats are treated. B overanchors on asthma. C overanchors on panic or metabolic explanation. D overanchors on heart failure and excludes pulmonary disease too quickly.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:52.

title: Tactical Extraction Versus Routine Care
stem: A casualty in tactical field care is conscious with suspected femur fracture and weak distal pulses. The threat is intermittent, extraction to a secure ambulance is 4 minutes away, and the team can apply rapid hemorrhage control. What is the best priority?

options.A: Complete splinting, intravenous access, and full vital signs before extraction if the intermittent threat appears briefly controlled.
options.B: Balance immediate life-saving care against extraction, deferring nonessential immobilization or intravenous therapy until safer conditions.
options.C: Move immediately without hemorrhage control because warm-zone care should wait until the casualty reaches the ambulance.
options.D: Attempt advanced airway management before extraction because tactical care prioritizes procedures before movement.

rationale: The best priority is to treat immediately correctable life threats while avoiding nonessential delays in an unsafe environment. A applies routine trauma sequencing despite tactical risk. C skips a rapid life-saving intervention. D prioritizes an advanced procedure that the scenario does not support.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:47.

topic: Contingency and crisis standards of care
title: Crisis Standards Legal Boundary
stem: During a pandemic surge, an EMS chief says crisis standards let the agency ignore all usual consent, documentation, and scope rules. Which medical director response is most accurate?

options.A: Crisis standards apply automatically whenever call volume exceeds staffed ambulances.
options.B: Any EMS medical director can declare crisis standards for their agency if patient demand is high.
options.C: Only an executive governmental declaration can alter legal standards, and it is not blanket permission to ignore law.
options.D: Crisis standards remove the need for documentation because care decisions are resource-based.

rationale: Crisis standards require formal governmental action and do not erase all usual legal obligations. A makes crisis standards automatic. B gives authority to the agency medical director alone. D incorrectly removes documentation duties.
citation: Catlett CL, Jenkins JL, Millin MG. Disaster preparedness and management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:290.

topic: Cleaning and disinfection of the ambulance
title: Ambulance Decontamination PPE
stem: After transporting a patient with copious vomiting and suspected infectious gastroenteritis, crews must clean contaminated equipment and the ambulance module. Which protection is most appropriate?

options.A: Bare hands are acceptable after patient transfer because the exposure is environmental rather than patient contact.
options.B: A surgical mask alone is sufficient because the task occurs after the patient has left the vehicle.
options.C: Respirator-only protection is preferred because surface contamination is mainly airborne after transport.
options.D: Gloves plus facial and eye protection, with gown-level protection when contamination of clothing is likely.

rationale: Cleaning a contaminated ambulance requires protection from contact and splash, including gloves and face/eye protection, with gown-level protection when clothing contamination is likely. A ignores environmental exposure. B protects incompletely. C overfocuses on airborne risk while missing contact and splash.
citation: MacDonald RD. Infectious and communicable diseases. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:210.

topic: Specialty hospital designations and patient transport
title: Specialty Designation Authority
stem: A state trauma system restricts trauma-center designation to hospitals meeting objective standards. A hospital argues the limitation is anticompetitive. What legal structure most directly protects the designation system?

options.A: Explicit statutory authority for the specialty designation system.
options.B: The Federal Emergency Trauma Access and Competition Act, which purportedly preempts antitrust challenges to state trauma-center designation.
options.C: EMS medical director preference documented in local destination protocols.
options.D: A memorandum from the largest hospital promising to accept transfers.

rationale: Explicit statutory authority is the strongest protection for a specialty designation system that limits participation based on standards. B sounds like a federal preemption theory but is not the relevant authority for this state designation structure. C is local preference rather than legal authority. D is a hospital promise, not a designation framework.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:35.

title: Cost-Minimization Analysis
stem: Two EMS antiemetic protocols have equivalent safety and symptom-control outcomes in local data. The agency wants to know which protocol costs less to deliver. Which economic analysis best fits?

options.A: Cost-effectiveness analysis comparing cost per additional successful treatment.
options.B: Cost-minimization analysis comparing costs when outcomes are equivalent.
options.C: Cost-benefit analysis converting all benefits into monetary terms.
options.D: Cost-utility analysis using quality-adjusted life-years.

rationale: Cost-minimization compares costs when outcomes are equivalent. A is for differing effectiveness. C monetizes benefits. D uses quality-adjusted life-years, which are unnecessary when outcomes are already equivalent.
citation: Osei-Ampofo M, Agyei-Baffour P. Cost analysis research. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:495.

topic: Regulations and standards (e.g., National Fire Protection Association [NFPA] 1582, Ryan White Act, Occupational Safety and Health Administration [OSHA] requirements, National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health [NIOSH])
title: Tuberculosis Exposure Program
stem: A crew transports a coughing patient later diagnosed with active pulmonary tuberculosis. The agency asks whether annual tuberculosis (TB) testing for everyone is enough. Which occupational-health approach is best?

options.A: Use surgical masks for crews because tuberculosis exposure is primarily a bloodborne-pathogen issue.
options.B: Use annual testing as the main program and add exposure-specific follow-up only after multiple personnel become symptomatic.
options.C: Use fit-tested respirators approved for airborne protection during suspected tuberculosis care and risk-based surveillance after exposure.
options.D: Use symptom screening as the main control measure and reserve fit testing for hospital-based clinicians.

rationale: Suspected pulmonary tuberculosis requires appropriate respiratory protection and a risk-based occupational-health program. A misclassifies tuberculosis as bloodborne. B underuses exposure-specific follow-up. D omits respirator fit testing for EMS personnel who may have airborne exposure.
citation: USSOCOM Office of the Surgeon General. Special Operations Advanced Tactical Paramedic Baseline Protocols. Version 14.04.2006.1617. 2026:94.

title: Alternative Destination Legal Check
stem: A state EMS office asks a medical director to start direct transport to a sobering center and urgent-care clinic. Neighboring states allow similar programs, but local regulations are unclear. What should happen first?

options.A: Start the program under medical-director authority because alternative destinations are clinical rather than legal decisions.
options.B: Use the neighboring state's statute as the operating standard until local law catches up.
options.C: Avoid all alternative destinations because only emergency departments can receive EMS patients.
options.D: Confirm state legal authority and regulatory limits before building protocols, training, and quality measures.

rationale: Alternative destination programs must fit state legal authority and regulation before protocols and training are built. A overstates medical-director authority. B imports another state law. C is too categorical because alternative destinations may be allowed in some systems.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:406.

title: Prehospital Surgical Airway Indication
stem: A facial trauma patient cannot be oxygenated by bag-valve-mask ventilation. Endotracheal intubation has failed twice, and supraglottic placement is impossible because of distorted anatomy. Which airway option best fits a rescue indication?

options.A: Perform a protocol-authorized cricothyrotomy by a credentialed clinician as a failed-airway rescue.
options.B: Continue repeated laryngoscopy attempts because surgical airway is never a prehospital option.
options.C: Transport without an airway because definitive airway attempts should wait for the operating room.
options.D: Place a nasopharyngeal airway and observe because facial trauma is the main indication for nasal rescue.

rationale: A credentialed, protocol-authorized cricothyrotomy is a rescue option when oxygenation and conventional airways fail. B rejects a possible rescue intervention. C leaves the patient without an airway. D is inappropriate in severe facial trauma and inadequate for failed oxygenation.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:35.

topic: Field reduction of dislocations, subluxations, or fractures
title: Wilderness Joint Reduction Tradeoff
stem: A wilderness team is 9 hours from extraction with a patient who has an anterior shoulder dislocation, intact distal perfusion, escalating pain, and no fracture signs. What makes field reduction a reasonable protocol consideration?

options.A: Reduce selected dislocations only after online medical control confirms no fracture by mechanism and exam.
options.B: Delayed evacuation changes the risk-benefit balance compared with urban EMS, so trained teams may reduce selected dislocations under protocol.
options.C: Avoid field reduction unless radiography is available, even when extraction delay is prolonged and perfusion is intact.
options.D: Reduce only during short transports, because complication management is closer than in delayed evacuations.

rationale: Long evacuation delays can shift the risk-benefit toward protocol-guided reduction by trained teams in selected patients. A adds an impractical imaging-level certainty requirement. C ignores wilderness transport delay. D reverses the logic by favoring reduction only when transport is short.
citation: Hawkins SC, Millin MG, Smith WR. Wilderness EMS. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:399.

topic: Regulations and standards (e.g., National Fire Protection Association [NFPA] 1582, Ryan White Act, Occupational Safety and Health Administration [OSHA] requirements, National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health [NIOSH])
title: NFPA Department Physician Role
stem: A fire department asks whether a contracted urgent-care clinic can replace a designated department physician for firefighter medical oversight. Which requirement is most relevant?

options.A: Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) rules require every firefighter visit to occur at an emergency department.
options.B: National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) approval replaces the need for medical oversight if respirators are used.
options.C: National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 1582 requires a designated department physician and a comprehensive medical program addressing worker health and safety.
options.D: The Ryan White framework assigns firefighter wellness oversight to the receiving hospital infection preventionist.

rationale: NFPA 1582 emphasizes a designated department physician and comprehensive occupational medical program. A invents an emergency-department requirement. B misuses NIOSH approval. D confuses infection exposure notification with firefighter wellness oversight.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:94.

title: CQI Versus QA
stem: An airway review finds wide variation in preoxygenation setup. One leader wants to punish outliers; another wants to redesign equipment layout, training, and measurement. Which distinction is most accurate?

options.A: Quality assurance changes systems, while continuous quality improvement mainly checks whether individuals violated rules.
options.B: Quality assurance and continuous quality improvement are identical terms, so the distinction has no practical value.
options.C: Continuous quality improvement should be used only after litigation, while quality assurance is prospective.
options.D: Continuous quality improvement modifies systems to produce better results, while quality assurance emphasizes compliance with standards.

rationale: Continuous quality improvement redesigns systems to improve outcomes, while quality assurance focuses on compliance with standards. A reverses the terms. B erases a useful distinction. C incorrectly limits CQI to litigation.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:428.

title: Decompression Sickness Mechanism
stem: A diver ascends rapidly after a deep dive and later develops joint pain and neurologic symptoms. Which mechanism best explains decompression sickness?

options.A: Inert gas forms tissue or vascular bubbles when ascent outpaces safe off-gassing.
options.B: Cold water causes peripheral vasospasm, which traps oxygen in the joints after ascent.
options.C: Hyperventilation before diving causes carbon dioxide bubbles to form after surfacing.
options.D: Nitrogen is converted into carbon monoxide during rapid pressure changes.

rationale: Decompression sickness is caused by inert gas bubbles forming when off-gassing is inadequate during or after ascent. B describes cold exposure, not decompression sickness. C incorrectly blames carbon dioxide bubbles. D invents a nitrogen-to-carbon-monoxide conversion.
citation: Couturier K, Husain I. Diving injury. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:423, 426.

title: Event Treatment Facility Design
stem: A music festival medical plan places the treatment tent behind a restricted loading dock with no public signage, limited shade, and no private exam space. Which revision most directly addresses treatment-facility design?

options.A: Keep the location hidden so only staff can find it and low-acuity visitors do not overwhelm the tent.
options.B: Move to an accessible, announced location with weather protection, patient privacy, and the highest-level provider present.
options.C: Use roaming crews only because fixed treatment sites are unnecessary at planned events.
options.D: Place the tent at the farthest edge of the venue so ambulances can leave without crossing pedestrian traffic.

rationale: Event treatment facilities should be accessible, announced, weather-protected, private, and appropriately staffed. A hides care access. C removes a useful fixed treatment site. D may help egress but sacrifices patient access and facility design.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:315.

title: Housing Status Documentation
stem: A charting committee notices that crews document housing instability as "frequent flyer," "street person," or leave it blank. Which documentation policy best supports clinical and operational use?

options.A: Discourage housing-status documentation because it may stigmatize patients.
options.B: Use informal crew language because it captures local knowledge better than structured fields.
options.C: Document housing status consistently and respectfully when it affects referral, risk, or system planning.
options.D: Record housing status only when police request it because homelessness is primarily a law-enforcement concern.

rationale: Consistent, respectful documentation can support clinical risk recognition, referral, and system planning. A loses relevant clinical context. B preserves stigmatizing informal language. D wrongly treats housing status as primarily a law-enforcement issue.
citation: Abramson TM, Burner E, Arora S, Wenzel S, Gausche-Hill M. Prehospital care for persons experiencing homelessness: a cross-sectional survey of the challenges, experiences, and perspectives of operational EMS agency medical directors. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2024;28(8):998-1005. doi:10.1080/10903127.2024.2358146. pp 998-1001.

topic: Use of capnometry and capnography waveforms in diagnosis and management
title: Sepsis Low End-Tidal Carbon Dioxide Response
stem: A spontaneously breathing patient with suspected sepsis is febrile, confused, tachycardic, and hypotensive. Oxygen saturation is 97% on supplemental oxygen, respiratory rate is 28/min, the waveform is present, and end-tidal carbon dioxide is 21 mm Hg. The crew asks what respiratory intervention should be used to correct the low end-tidal carbon dioxide. What is the best answer?

options.A: Coach the patient to slow breathing until end-tidal carbon dioxide normalizes, because the value primarily reflects respiratory alkalosis.
options.B: Begin bag-valve-mask ventilation solely to raise end-tidal carbon dioxide into the normal range.
options.C: Apply continuous positive airway pressure because low end-tidal carbon dioxide in sepsis indicates inadequate airway pressure.
options.D: Treat the underlying shock or sepsis and monitor ventilation; do not use a respiratory intervention solely to normalize end-tidal carbon dioxide.

rationale: In this context, low end-tidal carbon dioxide is more likely a marker of shock, compensation, or metabolic stress than a primary ventilation target. A could worsen compensation by forcing slower breathing. B is not indicated solely to normalize the number when ventilation is adequate. C misapplies continuous positive airway pressure to a perfusion and disease-process signal.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:63.

topic: High-risk delivery of a fetus (e.g., preterm delivery)
title: High-Risk Delivery Resources
stem: A 31-week pregnant patient is contracting every 2 minutes. The presenting part appears breech, and the nearest ambulance is already on scene with one paramedic and one emergency medical technician (EMT). What should the crew do early?

options.A: Request additional resources early because preterm delivery and abnormal presentation increase maternal and neonatal risk.
options.B: Transport alone because additional responders usually delay childbirth care.
options.C: Wait until delivery occurs before requesting help so resources can be matched to the infant's condition.
options.D: Cancel neonatal resources if maternal vital signs are normal because fetal risk is low when the mother is stable.

rationale: Preterm labor with breech presentation should prompt early additional maternal and neonatal resources. B underestimates risk. C waits until the predictable need has already arrived. D falsely treats normal maternal vital signs as reassuring for fetal or delivery risk.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:361.

title: Mass Gathering Drill Participation
stem: A stadium holds a tabletop exercise for a mass casualty plan, but dispatch, security, event medical staff, transport agencies, and receiving hospitals are absent. The after-action report says the plan is validated. What is the main flaw?

options.A: Tabletop exercises are invalid for mass gathering planning and must be replaced by a full-scale exercise every time.
options.B: The exercise cannot meaningfully validate coordination if key participating agencies and operational roles are absent.
options.C: Hospitals should be excluded because the event plan ends when patients leave the venue.
options.D: Dispatch should be excluded so field units can practice independent decision-making.

rationale: An exercise cannot validate coordination if major participating agencies are absent. A is too categorical; tabletop exercises can be useful. C excludes hospitals despite downstream surge and destination effects. D removes dispatch from a function it must coordinate.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:280.

title: Treatment Sector Function
stem: At a mass casualty incident, triage is underway and transport units are still organizing. Several red and yellow patients are waiting in a treatment area. Which task best fits the treatment sector?

options.A: Assign final hospital destinations for every patient before any immediate interventions are performed.
options.B: Stop triage operations and convert all personnel to transport loading.
options.C: Provide initial interventions for critical and noncritical patients while they await transport to definitive care.
options.D: Limit care to documentation because treatment should occur only after hospital arrival.

rationale: The treatment sector provides initial care to critical and noncritical patients while transport is organized. A overprioritizes destination assignment before needed care. B collapses separate incident functions. D withholds field care that the treatment area is designed to provide.
citation: Millin MG, Jenkins JL, Kirsch TD. Mass casualty management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:311.

title: Mass Gathering Initial Resource Report
stem: A grandstand collapses at a planned event. The first medical supervisor must give an initial report to dispatch before the exact injury count is known. Which report is most useful at this stage?

options.A: About 25 injured patients; media staging is near the west entrance; the grandstand was inspected last month; send the event manager to command.
options.B: Blocked access from the east gate and extrication needed; venue capacity is 14,000; weather is clear; notify the mayor.
options.C: Possible electrical hazards and need for transport units; social media reports are spreading; reimbursement contacts may be needed later.
options.D: About 25 injured patients, blocked east-gate access, possible electrical hazards, and need for extrication and transport units.

rationale: The most useful initial report includes the estimated casualty load, access limitations, hazards, and specific resource needs. A includes a casualty estimate but omits hazards, access, and resource needs while adding nonessential details. B includes access and one resource need but omits casualty estimate and hazards while adding low-priority information. C includes hazards and transport need but omits casualty estimate, access, and extrication need while adding later administrative concerns.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:269.

title: Wearable Defibrillator During CPR
stem: During cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR), a patient's wearable cardioverter defibrillator (WCD) announces that a shock is imminent. The patient is unconscious. What should rescuers do?

options.A: Stop compressions long enough to allow the WCD to deliver the announced shock, then resume CPR promptly.
options.B: Continue compressions through the WCD shock because it is lower energy than an external defibrillator.
options.C: Remove the WCD immediately and wait for EMS defibrillator pads before any shock occurs.
options.D: Disable the WCD because implanted and wearable defibrillators do not treat cardiac arrest rhythms.

rationale: If a wearable defibrillator announces a shock for an unconscious patient, rescuers should pause compressions briefly, allow the shock, and resume cardiopulmonary resuscitation promptly. B risks rescuer shock exposure. C delays a pending shock. D falsely claims wearable defibrillators cannot treat arrest rhythms.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:118.

title: Nontransporting ALS In Treatment Areas
stem: At a mass casualty incident, several advanced life support (ALS) ambulances are tied up providing care in the treatment area while transport queues grow. Nontransporting ALS units are available. What assignment best preserves evacuation capacity?

options.A: Keep transport-capable ALS ambulances in treatment until every critical patient has an ALS attendant assigned.
options.B: Use nontransporting ALS resources in treatment areas when appropriate so transport-capable ALS assets can move patients to definitive care.
options.C: Send nontransporting ALS resources to staging only because they cannot contribute without transport capability.
options.D: Cancel ALS treatment resources because mass casualty operations should prioritize transport over all on-site interventions.

rationale: Nontransporting advanced life support resources can staff treatment areas so transport-capable assets can move patients. A traps ambulances on scene. C wastes useful clinical resources. D overcorrects by canceling treatment support.
citation: Millin MG, Jenkins JL, Kirsch TD. Mass casualty management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:311.
